_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Queen Anne's County, MD. Records from 1706, the county's founding. Centreville is the oldest county seat on the Eastern Shore. No catastrophic courthouse fire, though some early records show deterioration from age. The county's records are complemented by strong church records — particularly the Quaker records from the Eastern Shore meetings.
display_name: Queen Anne's County, Maryland — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Centreville, MD — 100 Court House Square (expanded and renovated; the 1876 building is the core structure)

previous_courthouses.location: Queenstown (original county seat). Courts met in Queenstown, which was the commercial center on the Chester River.
previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved to Centreville in 1782 (courts moved by 1792). Records transferred. Queenstown remained a steamboat landing.

previous_courthouses.location: Centreville — frame and later brick courthouse on the courthouse square.
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current brick courthouse in 1876. Records survived.

record_losses.cause: Age-related deterioration and incomplete early record-keeping
records_destroyed: some early court records are deteriorated and difficult to read
some fragmentary records from the first two decades
records_survived: court minutes from the 1720s onward are detailed
record_losses.description: Queen Anne's County's records have no catastrophic gaps. The earliest records (1706-1720s) show some deterioration from age, and some are fragmentary. From the 1720s onward, the documentary record is continuous and well-maintained. The county seat move from Queenstown to Centreville in the 1780s-1790s did not result in record loss.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} was in Queen Anne's County after 1720, the records are likely to be available and readable. The earliest two decades require careful handling but are not lost.
record_losses.source: Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.

deeds.completeness: Good. Land records from the county's founding in 1706.
deeds.gaps: Some deterioration in the earliest volumes. Substantially complete from 1720 onward.
deeds.repository: Maryland State Archives; Queen Anne's County Circuit Court, Centreville

wills_estates.repository: Maryland State Archives; Queen Anne's County Register of Wills

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1780. Earlier marriages documented in church records.
marriages.repository: Maryland State Archives; Queen Anne's County Clerk of Court

court_minutes.completeness: Good from the 1720s onward. Some early records fragmentary.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in the first two decades (1706-1720).
court_minutes.online_access: Maryland State Archives; Archives of Maryland Online

vital_records.repository: Maryland Division of Vital Records; Maryland State Archives

substitute_sources.repository: Maryland Diocesan Archives; Maryland State Archives
substitute_sources.description: Vestry books, baptism and burial registers for the established Anglican parish. St. Paul's predates the county — the parish was established while the territory was still part of Talbot County.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Very good. Vestry records document both church and civil functions for the colonial period.

substitute_sources.repository: Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College; Maryland State Archives
substitute_sources.description: Monthly meeting records for the Quaker communities in what became Queen Anne's County. Birth, marriage, death, and disciplinary records.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Excellent for Quaker families. The discipline records document disownment for slaveholding, which provides evidence of changing attitudes toward slavery in the Quaker community.

substitute_sources.source_type: Freedmen's Bureau Records — Queen Anne's County
substitute_sources.description: Records from the Bureau office serving Queen Anne's County. Labor contracts, marriage registrations, and complaints.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Important for Black families in the transition from slavery to freedom.

narrative_hooks: The Centreville courthouse holds Queen Anne's County's records from 1706 — the year the county was created from parts of Talbot and Kent — and {ancestor_name}'s paper trail begins with the first deed book.

The Quaker meetings in Queen Anne's County kept records with a rigor that shamed the county government — and if {ancestor_name} was a Friend, the monthly meeting recorded births, marriages, and moral lapses with equal precision.
The county seat moved from Queenstown to Centreville, and the records moved with it — {ancestor_name}'s deeds filed in one town were read in another, but nothing was lost.
No fire took Queen Anne's County's records — and {ancestor_name}'s presence in the county can be traced through deeds, wills, and court minutes from 1706 to the present without a gap.
source_references: Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.
Friends Historical Library. Tuckahoe Meeting Records Guide. Swarthmore College.
National Archives. Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands (Record Group 105).
